data.title: TG Therapeutics Director Sagar Lonial Sells Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameLonial Sagar
RelationshipDirector
TypeSell
Amount$672,268
SEC FilingForm 4

TG Therapeutics Director, Sagar Lonial, sold 20,852 shares of common stock on September 11, 2025, at a weighted average price of $32.24 per share, totaling $672,268. Following the transaction, Lonial directly owns 94,061 shares of TG Therapeutics. The sale was conducted to satisfy income tax obligations related to restricted stock.

SEC Filing: TG THERAPEUTICS, INC. [ TGTX ] - Form 4 - Sep. 12, 2025

data.title: Lowe's Companies EVP Sells Over $11 Million in Stock
data.description:
Reporter NameMcfarland Joseph Michael
RelationshipEVP, Stores
TypeSell
Amount$11,942,439
SEC FilingForm 4

Joseph Michael McFarland, EVP of Stores at Lowe's Companies, sold 43,810 shares of common stock on September 11, 2025. The transactions were executed at weighted average prices of $272.5651 and $273.1789, respectively, totaling $11,942,439 in sales. Following these transactions, McFarland directly owns 66,566 shares of Lowe's Companies.

SEC Filing: LOWES COMPANIES INC [ LOW ] - Form 4 - Sep. 12, 2025

data.title: Bank of America CEO Appoints Co-Presidents
data.description:
By Dean Seal

Bank of America's chief executive has appointed two co-presidents and given another title to the bank's chief financial officer.

The bank said Friday that Chair and CEO Brian Moynihan has tapped Dean Athanasia and Jim DeMare for the co-president roles. CFO Alastair Borthwick is now also the company's executive vice president.

Moynihan said in a letter to employees that Athanasia and DeMare would drive enterprise-wide initiatives focused on increasing the bank's market share and returns.

With his new EVP role, Borthwick will take on additional responsibilities, including Bank of America's global real estate portfolio, Moynihan said in the letter.

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com

data.title: US appeals court declines Google bid to further pause Play store overhaul in Epic Games case
data.description:
Alphabet's Google on Friday failed to persuade a U.S. appeals court to further freeze an order forcing it to make sweeping reforms to its app store Play while it challenges the decision in a lawsuit by "Fortnite" video game maker Epic Games.

The San Francisco-based 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals denied Google's request to continue its pause of the order, which requires the technology company to restore competition by allowing users to download rival app stores within its Play store and by making Play's app catalog available to those competitors, among other reforms.

The appeals court said Google failed to meet the high bar to keep the order on hold. Google still has up to 10 months to comply with some key provisions of the underlying injunction and 30 days for others.

The appeals court said in a separate order the full court would not revisit Google's appeal. Google could ask the U.S. Supreme Court to intervene.

Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Epic Games CEO Tim Sweeney, in a post on the social media platform X, praised the court's order and said developers and consumers would soon benefit from it.

Epic in its 2020 lawsuit accused Google of monopolizing how consumers access apps on Android devices and pay for transactions within apps. The Cary, North Carolina-based company convinced a San Francisco jury in 2023 that Google illegally stifled competition.

U.S. District Judge James Donato in San Francisco issued his order in the case last year requiring Google to overhaul the Play store. Google has denied any wrongdoing.

Donato's injunction said that for three years, Google cannot prohibit the use of in-app payment methods and must allow users to download competing third-party Android app platforms or stores.

The order also restricts Google from making payments to device makers to preinstall its app store and from sharing revenue generated from the Play store with other app distributors.

In upholding the injunction, a 9th Circuit panel in July said the record in Epic's lawsuit was "replete with evidence that Google's anticompetitive conduct entrenched its dominance."

Google told the appeals court in a filing on August 8 that Donato's injunction was "unprecedented" and would put Google and rival Apple on uneven playing fields.

Epic mostly lost a similar lawsuit it filed against Apple in 2020, accusing it of monopolizing app distribution and payments.

Google said if the jury verdict and Donato's order are left in place, Google and Apple will "operate under different legal rules from two conflicting decisions of this court."

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1438 ET - Adobe's F3Q results mark a turning point, with key growth metrics in its core and new AI-first products trending positively, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. The report should help more investors see Adobe as an AI beneficiary, though a debate over whether it's an AI winner or loser is likely to continue, the analysts say. Going forward, gradual, continued traction with AI-first products will likely drive reaccelerating growth, the analysts say, estimating an incremental 1% point of topline growth in fiscal 2027, assuming it maintains its current quarterly AI net new annual recurring revenue run rate. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1426 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery makes sense strategically, but a full integration would take time, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. They estimate that integration and restructuring under the combined company would likely take years to implement, pointing to the lengthy process that came with Warner Bros.' combination with Discovery. That said, the deal would significantly boost Paramount's prospects, particularly in film and television, given access to Warner Bros.' best-in-class library, they say. It would also create a formidable competitor in streaming with the potential combination of Paramount+ and HBO Max, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1419 ET - 2025 will mark the end of one of Bank of America's biggest investment themes for this decade: the trend of moving away from Chinese assets known as the "Anywhere But China" trade, the bank's analysts say in a research note. Amid the AI bubble in the U.S., investors should take a "barbell" approach to investing, with capital flowing to a mix of low-risk and high-risk global technology investments, the analysts say. China and Japan are bright spots in this new paradigm, they say. Chinese technology companies are an optimal way to barbell the U.S. AI bubble, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1121 ET - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery would likely clear regulatory scrutiny, according to Benchmark. The timing is "particularly auspicious" says analyst Daniel Kurnos. He says the FCC's September preliminary agenda seeks to potentially eliminate the rule prohibiting dual ownership of broadcast networks. While CBS is the only broadcast network and station group involved in this potential deal, it "suggests an openness to further media consolidation of the major players," Kurnos says. The Ellison family's close family ties with the Trump Administration will likely also help avoid potential obstacles to the merger, Benchmark says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1040 ET - A Paramount Skydance/Warner Bros. Discovery merger "could yield meaningful upside for the combined entity," says Benchmark analyst Daniel Kurnos. Two years ago, Benchmark estimated a merger between the two companies would create synergies between $3.15 billion and $4.9 billion, although the companies have since begun their own cost reduction programs. "The math remains relatively the same, and the industrial logic has only gotten stronger in what appears to be a consolidating media landscape," Kurnos says. The analyst adds that even without synergies, "Paramount could pay $18 per share for Warner and still have the deal be value neutral or possibly even marginally accretive." Warner Bros. Discovery surges 12%, while Paramount Skydance is up 4%. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0920 ET - A Paramount Skydance merger with Warner Bros. Discovery could get more difficult if Paramount doesn't act quickly, says Benchmark analyst Matthew Harrigan. The Warner Bros. Discovery separation is planned for 2Q26, which would make a Paramount bid for the company tax challenged for 12 months, "providing an impetus for a serious near-term offer," Harrigan says. The separate Warner Bros. and Discovery Global stocks could also get more expensive as Harrigan says investors are currently undervaluing the company's earnings potential with the Studio and HBO Max divisions set for renewed growth in the wake of an eventual $5 billion in cost reductions. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0907 ET - Sony, Netflix, Apple and Amazon could all be potential bidders for parts of Warner Bros. Discovery, but another suitor is unlikely to challenge Paramount Skydance's acquisition bid, says Benchmark analyst Matthew Harrigan. Paramount Skydance has privileged access to capital and could create more synergies with a merger than other potential bidders. The company is also best positioned to manage the decline in linear television networks while transitioning to streaming distribution. Sony is unlikely to challenge an Ellison family bid, Netflix is wary of old media influences and Apple and Amazon could be turned off by share price impact, Harrigan says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0825 ET - Mizuho analysts say in a research note that they expect Applied Materials to lose market share. The semiconductor-equipment maker's revenue is largely exposed to segments that are facing increased competition from China, the analysts say. As a result, Applied Materials is losing customers. This comes after the company last month forecast a drop in profit and revenue this quarter, hurt by challenges in China and choppy demand across end markets. Mizuho downgrades Applied Materials to neutral and lowers its price target to $175 from $200. Applied Materials is off 2% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0759 ET - Adobe logs better-than-expected F3Q results, with its small-but-quickly growing AI-infused product portfolio hitting its annual recurring revenue goal a quarter early, KeyBanc says. "The outlook for next quarter is moving higher, but we are not yet certain the green-shoots are there for a full-fledged, material acceleration in FY26 at this point," analysts Jackson Ader and Jack Nichols say in a research note. While there are many AI model builders developing their own potentially competitive tools according to the analysts, they say Adobe nevertheless remains the center of gravity for the creative process. Adobe shares gain 3% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0659 ET - Adobe is fighting an uphill battle, attempting to prove to investors that AI isn't a threat to its core value proposition. The software company did so in its latest quarter, Davidson analysts say in a research note, as it outperformed Wall Street expectations and effectively drove adoption of its expanding AI-infused product portfolio. This momentum around usage and adoption supports a boost to its outlook, they add. "The company also continues to actively buy back shares at current levels and deliver mid-40% operating margins, supporting double digits earnings growth," the analysts write. Davidson maintains its buy rating, and Adobe shares climb 3.8% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0656 ET - Adobe's beat-and-raise in its fiscal 3Q doesn't ease long-term concerns, Melius Research analysts say in a note. "The story at the end of the day is revenue growth could decelerate next year," they write, adding that the software company's 4Q guide calls for an on-year slowdown in sales despite price hikes. Adobe benefited from higher prices in its latest quarter, but pricing doesn't seem sustainable given increasing competition. Management notes that competition from Figma, Canva and bigger technology companies such as Meta Platforms doesn't seem to be affecting the lower-end of the market yet. Melius maintains its sell rating on the stock. Adobe shares rise 3.6%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0011 ET - Service Stream's bulls at Macquarie are enthused by the infrastructure-services provider's A$1.6 billion defense contract, telling clients that it opens up a whole new vertical for the Australian company. Macquarie analysts write in a note that they hadn't expected Service Stream to win in two regions, or for the award to be this large. The contract represents a 10% increase to their prior fiscal 2027 revenue forecast and puts Service Stream in a good position to secure additional work that drive incremental revenue growth, they add. Macquarie lifts its target price 12% to A$2.70 and keeps an outperform rating on the stock, which is down 0.7% at A$2.305. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1006 ET - Roughly half--48%--of newly built apartments completed in 1Q were rented within three months, according to Redfin. That is up from 47% in 4Q 2024 and 46% the quarter before that. While the recent uptick in the rental absorption rate is small, it's notable because for much of 2021-2023, the absorption rate was declining. It's likely inching up now because the number of new apartments hitting the market is falling. Renters have fewer options to choose from, making it easier for landlords to fill units fast. As many Americans find themselves priced out of homeownership, rents are beginning to rise again. Apartment building permits have fallen below pre-pandemic levels. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0929 ET - The current offer put forward by BBVA is sufficiently attractive for Sabadell shareholders to accept it, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. BBVA, which last year launched a hostile bid for its smaller rival, opened the acceptance period to Sabadell shareholders earlier this week. Sabadell's board has recommended that shareholders don't tender their shares. "Both banks are arriving at very different answers and, in reality, the answer is probably somewhere in the middle," analyst Benjamin Toms writes. There is potential to create value out of a deal even if it isn't as large or quickly realizable as BBVA estimates, he notes. To avoid being left with an acceptance rate between 30% and 50%, BBVA could still boost the price of the offer by around 10%, he calculates. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0829 ET - Italian banks are in a good place with loan and fee growth picking up and consolidation scenarios still to play out, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. "The Italian banks continue to live some of their best lives, with strong [returns on tangible equity] and high payout ratios, but also dynamic management teams that continue to work hard to improve their businesses," analysts Hugo Cruz and Ben Maher write after a trip to Milan. They point to more evidence of a pick up in corporate loan growth and stable or easing loan pricing competition, supporting net interest income trends. The market might need more clarity on the outcome of Monte dei Paschi's Mediobanca takeover before another potential M&A round, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0624 ET - The current price that BBVA is offering for Sabadell is insufficient, says a Sabadell board member that represents its third largest shareholder. Fintech Advisory holds a 3.86% stake in the Spanish bank. "In my opinion, the transaction presented by BBVA is the right strategy for both institutions, although at a price that currently makes it unfeasible," David Martinez Guzman says. He adds that he agrees with the board's rejection of the offer and won't tender the shares he owns or those he manages through the fund. BBVA should reconsider and submit a competitive offer that will allow it to get at least 50% of Sabadell shareholders on board, he says. Sabadell issued its formal opinion on the bid and is recommending that shareholders don't tender their shares to the $18 billion takeover offer. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0515 ET - The Indonesian government's plan to inject IDR200 trillion into state-owned banks may ease investor concerns over liquidity conditions and rising fund costs, says Nomura analyst Tushar Mohata. The cash injection would boost sector-wide excess liquidity, meaning deposits minus loans, by 37%. That would be good for major lenders, whose shares have been under persistent selling pressure, with valuations well below historical averages, Mohata adds. Nomura thinks SOE banks, particularly Bank Rakyat Indonesia, Bank Mandiri and Bank Negara Indonesia are well-positioned to deploy new liquidity into higher-margin segments, while Bank Syariah Indonesia needs more funding discipline. It retains buy calls on all four. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Indonesia's plan to inject 200 trillion rupiah into state-owned banks isn't likely to meaningfully lift loan growth, DBS Group Research says. Overall loan demand is likely weakening, with investment loan demand showing no signs of accelerating and working capital demand still soft. Banks are also expected to stay cautious on consumer loans amid asset-quality concerns. "Given the lack of structural demand-side support, we do not expect this policy to significantly change the credit growth trajectory," they say. DBS reiterates a neutral rating on Indonesia's banking sector and prefers Bank Jago and Bank Central Asia. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2148 ET - Singapore banks' net interest margin are expected to be pressured this year and 1H of 2026 amid low interest rates and liquidity inflows, UOB Kay Hian analyst Jonathan Koh writes in a report. "Singapore has become a magnet for liquidity seeking shelter," and this is expected to impact the banks, he says. The Singapore Overnight Rate Average--a benchmark for pricing floating-rate mortgage rates--recently dropped to a low of 0.73% amid Indonesia's social unrest and series of demonstrations in late August. The stubbornly low domestic interest rates, which look to be structural in nature, are likely to keep NIMs compressed, Koh adds. UOB KH downgrades Singapore's banking sector to market weight.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2121 ET - DBS Group's "very decent" 2026 dividend yield of 5.1% could rise further on potential capital-return dividends, RHB Research writes in an email. RHB Research estimates the Singapore lender will pay out a 2026 dividend of S$2.70, based on DBS's guidance that its S$0.24 increase in annual dividends should be sustainable next year. Investors are likely to welcome such strong dividend visibility, RHB Research adds. The analyst expects DBS to continue committing to capital returns and dividends, but projects the bank's 2025 profit after tax and minority interests will fall 5% on year. DBS's shares surpassed S$53 on Thursday to notch a new intraday record. The stock trades 0.1% lower at S$52.52. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2053 ET - Indonesia's planned 200 trillion rupiah fund injection is expected to benefit state-owned lenders, Citi analyst Ferry Wong says in a note. Six banks are expected to receive the liquidity, including Bank Rakyat Indonesia, Bank Mandiri, Bank Negara Indonesia and Bank Tabungan Negara, he notes. The funds, drawn from the government's surplus at Bank Indonesia, equal about 4.5% of total deposits at these state-owned enterprise banks, he reckons. With potential funding costs below market deposit rates, lenders should see improved lending margins, boosting profitability, he adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1927 ET [Dow Jones]--Any potential upside to Macquarie's earnings from rebounding capital markets already looks priced in, according to Morgan Stanley analysts. They tell clients in a note that recovering M&A activity presents upside risk to their forecasts, but that the stock is trading at 21.5 times their fiscal 2026 net profit forecast, which compares with a 10-year average of 16. This suggests to them that a capital-markets recovery and Macquarie's structural growth options are already incorporated into investors' outlook. Return-on-equity prospects are also weaker than at Commonwealth Bank, they add. MS keeps an equal-weight rating and A$216.00 target price on the stock, which is at A$219.91 ahead of the open. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1523 ET - Oil futures post weekly gains with geopolitical risk premium outweighing oversupply concerns as the Russia-Ukraine war continues unabated and an Israeli strike aimed at Hamas leaders in Qatar threatens to increase tensions in the Middle East. The market took in stride OPEC+ plans to continue unwinding output cuts in October, while another unexpected weekly U.S. crude stock build was seen as bearish. Additional U.K. sanctions against Russia and U.S. calls for Europe to hit India and China with tariffs for buying Russian oil gave prices a lift at the end of the week. WTI settles up 0.5% at $62.69 for a 1.3% weekly gain, and Brent rises 0.9% to $66.99, up 2.3% from a week ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1516 ET - Oil futures post weekly gains with geopolitical risk premium outweighing oversupply concerns as the Russia-Ukraine war continues unabated and an Israeli strike aimed at Hamas leaders in Qatar threatens to increase tensions in the Middle East. The market took in stride OPEC+ plans to continue unwinding output cuts in October, while another unexpected weekly U.S. crude stock build was seen as bearish. Additional U.K. sanctions against Russia and U.S. calls for Europe to hit India and China with tariffs for buying Russian oil gave prices a lift at the end of the week. WTI settles up 0.5% at $62.69 for a 1.3% weekly gain, and Brent rises 0.9% to $66.99, up 2.3% from a week ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1433 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose for a third consecutive week, increasing by two to 416, or 72 fewer than a year ago, Baker Hughes reports. Rigs directed at natural gas were unchanged this week at 118, up by 21 from a year earlier. The EIA expects drilling activity in 2026 to be more centered in natural gas-intensive regions given its forecasts for lower oil but higher natural gas prices. "We forecast that crude oil will trade at its lowest premium to natural gas since 2005," the EIA said in its latest Short Term Energy Outlook. "We expect decreases in natural gas produced as a by-product of oil directed drilling will offset increases in that produced by natural gas-directed drilling." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1033 ET - Oil futures are recovering from the previous day's losses as the U.K. announces new sanctions on Russia and the U.S. urges Europe to put tariff pressure on buyers of Russian oil, namely India and China. "The Russia/Ukraine war remains a positive to crude prices with Ukraine hitting a major oil export facility in Russia late yesterday," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Russia exports nearly 7 million barrels a day, therefore any disruptions whether from tariffs or war conflicts could be a bullish catalyst for crude prices near-term." WTI is up 1.5% at $63.28 a barrel and Brent is up 1.6% at $67.43. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0908 ET - Equinor faces challenges from an expected decline in European gas prices and lower financial flexibility due to its participation in Orsted's rights issue, UBS analysts write. European gas storage has refilled steadily, while at the same time new LNG projects have started up. Longer term, LNG investments are picking up and UBS expects gas prices to drop even further than previously. Meanwhile, the Danish wind-farm developer has confirmed it will participate in the Orsted rights issue to maintain its 10% stake, at a cost of $939 million. "This will further increase gearing and reduce financial flexibility, increasing risks around shareholder returns into next year." UBS cuts its price target on the stock to 210 kroner ($21) from 220 kroner and reiterates its sell rating. Shares rise 0.5% to 242.50 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0342 ET - European oil stocks slip in opening trade as worries about oversupply continue to mount. Brent crude trades down 0.7% at $65.93 a barrel and prices remain under pressure. A surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels in the U.S. last week combined with growing concerns around oversupply as OPEC+ members plan to boost production is contributing to traders' worries, Derren Nathan, head of equity research at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. In London, BP shares fall 0.7% and Shell drops 0.15%. France's TotalEnergies is down 0.65% while shares in Spain's Repsol and Italy's Eni are down around 0.4%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0011 ET - Service Stream's bulls at Macquarie are enthused by the infrastructure-services provider's A$1.6 billion defense contract, telling clients that it opens up a whole new vertical for the Australian company. Macquarie analysts write in a note that they hadn't expected Service Stream to win in two regions, or for the award to be this large. The contract represents a 10% increase to their prior fiscal 2027 revenue forecast and puts Service Stream in a good position to secure additional work that drive incremental revenue growth, they add. Macquarie lifts its target price 12% to A$2.70 and keeps an outperform rating on the stock, which is down 0.7% at A$2.305. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0443 ET - Mining stocks have helped propel the FTSE 100 index to a record high in morning trade in London. Anglo American shares are up nearly 13% over the past 5 days on the news that it has agreed to merge with Teck Resources to create a $53 billion copper giant. Glencore is up 7%. Much of the share-price growth has been concentrated on gold and silver stocks as precious metals continue to benefit from safe-have demand. Fresnillo trades up 7%, Hochschild Mining is up 6%, with Endeavour Mining 4% higher. The FTSE 100 trades up 0.3% at 9,326 points. Over the year-to-date, it is up 14%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0327 ET - Miners lead the gains on the FTSE 100 in opening trade. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo tops the index, with shares trading around 4% higher. Others such as Anglo American, Endeavour Mining and Glencore rise around 2%. The rise comes as gold trades up 0.5% at $3,653 a troy ounce, while silver rises 1.3% to $42.1 an ounce. Gold is being boosted by bets from investors that the U.S. Federal Reserve starts cutting rates, a weaker dollar and continuing geopolitical tensions, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya writes. Thursday's economic data from the U.S. has given the Federal Reserve the green light to cut rates next week, Ozkardeskaya adds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0242 ET - UBS reckons gold will reach $3,800/oz by the end of 2025, up from a previous forecast of $3,500/oz. By mid-2026, the price could be around $3,900/oz, it reckons. The bank had previously projected $3,700/oz gold mid next year. UBS says expectations that the Federal Reserve will restart its easing cycle due to weak jobs data are fueling investor appetite. Ongoing geopolitical uncertainties are also underpinning demand, it says. UBS expects ETF holdings will rise near a previous record high of just above 3,900 metric tons by the end of 2025. UBS stays long gold in its global asset allocation, it says. Spot gold is up 0.5% at $3,651.49. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0101 ET - Aggregate global commodity prices are tracking--and will likely continue to track--broadly sideways, says HSBC economist Paul Bloxham. "However, this overall picture hides considerable divergence, volatility and market fragmentation below the surface," says Bloxham. He highlights record-high gold prices, volatile copper markets and softer oil prices, among other moves. "'Geoeconomics,' a nexus of geopolitics and economics, is increasingly driving commodity market dynamics" from gold and oil to soy beans and rare earths, says Bloxham. "More price volatility, shorter commodity price cycles and greater market fragmentation are the results," he says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2255 ET - Perenti's latest contract for underground mining services at Ramelius' Dalgaranga gold project pleases Citi, which reiterates a buy rating and A$2.55 target on the stock. "Positively, PRN continues to replenish and build on its robust secured work," analyst William Park says in a note. Citi estimates Perenti's work in hand could be as much as A$6 billion. The company also has plenty of room within existing capital expenditure guidance to chase new contract awards, Park adds. "We believe there is no shortage of opportunities that could potentially be crystallized in favor of PRN in FY26 including, but not limited to, Cowal, Geita, and Red Chris," he says. Perenti is down 1.0% at A$2.475, following a 4.2% gain Thursday. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1922 ET [Dow Jones]--Lithium miner Pilbara Minerals should deliver a relatively smooth operational performance in FY 2026, according to Jarden analysts Ben Lyons and Adam Bennett. The year ahead is "free of the major expansion tie-ins that impacted FY25, and with maintenance shutdowns scheduled on a quarterly basis," they say. The analysts reckon Pilbara Minerals will break even on a cash basis in FY 2026 and FY 2027 at a US$900/metric ton spodumene price. The stock remains Jarden's preferred Australian lithium exposure. The bank views it as a core holding in the resources sector, the analysts say. They reiterate an overweight rating and A$2.20 target. The stock last traded at A$1.97. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com)

1905 ET - Morgans's big takeaway from a visit to Aeris Resources' Tritton copper mine: the operation is worth more than it thought. Morgans now values Tritton at A$268 million, up from A$181 million before. That view is driven a shift in its assumptions from FY27 as processing of ore from the Constellation deposit starts. Morgans raises expectations for the copper head grade by 0.1% from FY27. "Given the copper grade at Constellation is 25% higher than the greater Tritton resources (ex Constellation) we think this is conservative," analyst Ross Bennett says. "In a scenario where head grade lifts to 1.9% copper we forecast 32,500 tons copper production in FY 2028/FY 2029." Morgans raises its price target on Aeris by 39%, to A$0.43/share. Aeris ended Thursday at A$0.325. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1514 ET - Rivian's recent recall adds another stumbling block for the electric carmaker. The company is recalling some 24,214 of its R1S and R1T electric vehicles due to a software defect that may cause its Highway Assist system to misclassify other vehicles, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration says. The recall comes as Rivian faces a number of hurdles, including the end of a $7,500 federal tax credit for EVs that expires at the end of the month. The company has also laid off workers as it comes under pressure to lower costs as it prepares for the rollout of its next model. Shares fall 3.5%, to $13.48. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1500 ET - Airlines are experiencing stronger demand in the fall rather than the summer, Deutsche analyst Michel Linenberg says. October looks better than September and September looks better than August, he says, citing booking trends and commentary from executives at a conference this week show. Strong premium demand has been a significant driver of this trend, as well as bounce-back in corporate travel after a slow period this spring. Other segments, such as the main cabin and domestic markets, remain sluggish. Airlines that depend on main cabin domestic sales are restructuring their networks, Linenberg says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1211 ET - Amazon Autos hasn't hit top gear yet, especially in used vehicles, say Davidson analysts Tom White and Wyatt Swanson. Amazon Autos was launched in 2023 with a partnership with Hyundai for new vehicles, but the analysts say that Hyundai dealers are still seeing very limited sales through Amazon. Early last month, Amazon said dealers would be able to list their used vehicles on Amazon Autos, but " there remains relatively scant Used inventory available on the Amazon Autos platform," the analysts say. Almost all of the used inventory that is available is from Hertz, which recently said it was partnering with Amazon, and participating Hyundai dealers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1126 ET - Tariffs were expected to hit the auto industry hard, "yet six months into the onset of tariffs, we've been positively surprised by the extent to which the industry has held in better than anticipated," Barclays analysts Dan Levy and Josh Cho say in a research note, citing minimal disruption to production and sales, as well as ongoing consumer strength. Barclays upgrades its sector view on the U.S. autos and mobility to neutral from negative, as tariffs are now well understood by the market and likely to ease with additional trade deals. General Motors and Aptiv are especially well positioned, the analysts note, upgrading each to overweight from equal weight. GM should benefit from easing electric-vehicle regulations and resiliency in pricing, while parts supplier Aptiv can benefit from improved business dynamics alongside reasonable valuation, the analysts say. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0857 ET - Mercedes-Benz could see some EBIT improvement by 2027, but shares have catalysts for improvement in 2026, Citi analysts write. EBIT margins have fallen by around 1,000 basis points on a combination of China market challenges, volume and pricing declines, U.S. tariffs, and the poor performance of previous EV investments. The company is now responding by cutting fixed and variable costs and a new S class model cycle should drive some on-year improvement in 2027, Citi adds. "We think Mercedes should commit to a fixed rather than a free cash flow-based annual return and restart the buyback to signal long-term confidence." Shares fall 1.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0833 ET - BMW said this week that some of its assumptions regarding second-half earnings had changed, which Citi analysts say will focus investor minds. A delay in signing the EU-U.S. tariff agreement means manufacturers are paying 27.5% rather than 15% on imports for longer--potentially costing BMW around 200 million euros in third-quarter EBIT, the bank estimates. More worrying is that BMW's China assumptions have again proven too optimistic, resulting in a sharper China sales decline in 2025 and dealer compensation payments hitting margins, Citi says. Despite cost cuts and a better performance in other regions, "the worry remains that BMW is no longer competitive in China and the deterioration continues to weigh on earnings going forward." Shares fall 1.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0803 ET - Transat A.T. faces a number of headwinds in the near-term, but TD Cowen's Tim James says in a note that he doesn't believe they should shift investor focus from attractive valuation when looking out over the next 12 months. The analyst says drags related to demand softness, increased competition and supply were themes in the F3Q report, but the airline's Elevation cost and revenue enhancement strategy, debt reduction, partnership with Porter airlines, along with improving demand "should allow Transat to generate structurally higher earnings despite short-term headwinds." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0420 ET - China Eastern Airlines' outlook could remain challenging, says DBS Group Research's Paul Yong in a note. He expects the airline's profitability to remain under strain as passenger yield pressures endure, driven by fare wars, muted business travel demand and fiercer competition from China's growing high-speed rail network. He lowers his 2025 and 2026 earnings forecasts for the airline by 47% and 52%, respectively. DBS trims its target on China Eastern's A shares to CNY4.10 from CNY4.30, and on its H shares to HK$2.90 from HK$3.10, while maintaining its hold rating. China Eastern's Shanghai-listed shares closed 1.2% lower at CNY4.03, while its Hong Kong-listed stock ended flat at HK$2.97. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

1840 ET - Risks to Mainfreight's earnings tilt to the downside, which may prolong the company's three-year cycle of profit downgrades, Forsyth Barr says. Mainfreight's pretax profit fell 24% in the first 17 weeks of FY 2026. That puts Mainfreight in catch-up mode, but analyst Andy Bowley says it becomes tougher to beat its performance a year ago as FY 2026 progresses. Forsyth Barr expects Mainfreight's annual pretax profit to total NZ$350 million. This implies the on-year decline narrows to 4% for the remaining 35 weeks. "We continue to believe this is achievable but requires better margin conversion, particularly in Transport and Warehousing, than that highlighted at the annual shareholder meeting, along with consistent new customer wins," Forsyth Barr says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Ekofisk weakens, WTI steady
data.description:
North Sea Ekofisk was offered lower in the Platts window on Friday, while U.S. WTI Midland was steady after a higher offer did not draw a higher bid.

PLATTS WINDOW

* Indications are free on board (FOB) unless marked as cost, insurance and freight (CIF) or delivered at place (DAP).

* No deals for benchmark grades, a trade source said.

* Ekofisk: Offered at dated Brent plus $1.05 by Mercuria for September 29-October 1, lower than an offer on Thursday.

* WTI Midland: Exxon Mobil offered October 14-18 at dated Brent plus $1.84 CIF Rotterdam. That was up from a trade on Thursday at dated plus $1.60 on a CIF Rotterdam basis, or dated Brent plus 57 cents on a FOB basis, according to Reuters calculations.

data.title: Gilead beats whistleblower lawsuit over hepatitis C drugs
data.description:
Key points:
  • Judge said no evidence to support claims of kickbacks on Harvoni, Sovaldi
  • Whistleblower will appeal, his lawyers say
  • Gilead settled claims of kickbacks on HIV drugs earlier this year

A federal judge in Pennsylvania rejected a former Gilead Sciences sales representative’s claims that the company paid kickbacks to doctors and patients to boost prescriptions of its hepatitis C drugs Sovaldi and Harvoni.

In a ruling on Thursday, U.S. District Judge Cynthia Rufe in Philadelphia sided with Gilead in the whistleblower lawsuit brought by its former employee Toby Travis, finding that Travis hadn’t proven his claims that Gilead used speaker programs and charitable donations to hide payments to doctors and patients meant to induce them to write and fill prescriptions for the drugs.

She also rejected his argument that Gilead’s April agreement to pay $202 million to settle a whistleblower lawsuit accusing it of paying kickbacks to doctors who agreed to prescribe its HIV drugs could bolster his claims, saying Gilead’s admissions in that case didn’t apply to its actions with Sovaldi and Harvoni.

James Miller, an attorney for Travis, said they plan to appeal the ruling and expect it to be reversed.

A spokesperson for Gilead said the company’s speaker programs serve to educate healthcare professionals about the medicines, and the donations to charitable foundations help patients access treatments they need.

Travis, who was a sales representative for Gilead’s hepatitis C drugs between 2013 and 2014, filed his whistleblower lawsuit in 2017. He accused the company of violating the anti-kickback statute and the False Claims Act, saying that prescriptions submitted to Medicare for reimbursement as a result of the kickbacks constituted fraud.

He claimed the company was using a sham speaker program, which hired healthcare providers to give presentations about its drugs, as a way to push doctors to write more prescriptions for its hepatitis C drugs and reward high prescribers. He also accused Gilead of using donations to a program meant to help low-income patients afford co-payments for drugs to induce those patients to use Harvoni and Sovaldi specifically.

In 2019, the U.S. declined to intervene in the action.

Gilead urged Rufe to throw out Travis’ claims, claiming he had no evidence to support them. The company also asked Rufe to sanction Travis, accusing him of knowingly trying to defraud the court by pursuing baseless claims.

In her ruling on Thursday, Rufe declined to sanction Travis but agreed that despite years of discovery, his evidence was “flimsy.” There was no evidence that Gilead was hiring specific doctors based on their prescription numbers, she said, and the donations Gilead made to the charitable foundation were far larger than what the foundation spent on the company’s hepatitis C drugs.

The case is United States v. Gilead Sciences Inc, U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania, No. 2:17-cv-01183.

For Travis: James Shah, Natalie Finkelman, Bruce Parke, Alec Berin, James Miller and Nathan Zipperian of Miller Shah; David Caputo and John Crutchlow of Youman & Caputo; and John Gross of the Weiser Law Firm

For Gilead: Molly Flynn of Barnes & Thornburg; Devora Allon, Jay Lefkowitz, Kevin Neylan and Kevin Jonke of Kirkland & Ellis; and Will Sears of Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan

data.title: TSX posts sixth straight weekly gain ahead of expected central bank rate cuts
data.description:
Key points:
  • TSX ends down 0.4% at 29,283.82
  • Industrials lose 1%, tech ends 0.8% lower
  • For the week, the index advances 0.8%

Canada's main stock gave back some of its weekly gain on Friday, with declines for technology and industrial shares, as investors braced for interest rate decisions next week by the Bank of Canada and the Federal Reserve.

The S&P/TSX composite index ended down 124.07 points, or 0.4%, at 29,283.82, pulling back from a record closing high on Thursday.

For the week, the index advanced 0.8%, its sixth straight weekly gain as gold's record-setting run bolstered metal mining shares.

"It's still a momentum-driven market," said Michael Dehal, a senior portfolio manager at Dehal Investment Partners at Raymond James.

"We could see some volatility next week ... If we do correct for the next couple of weeks after the Fed announcement, the BoC announcement, I do think investors will probably snap up those equities going into Q4."

Both the Bank of Canada and the Federal Reserve are expected to resume their easing campaigns next Wednesday. The Canadian central bank has been on hold since lowering its benchmark rate to 2.75% in March.

Technology fell 0.8%, industrials (.GSPTTIN) lost 1% and heavily weighted financials ended 0.5% lower.

Three of 10 major sectors avoided declines, with energy barely changed as the price of oil settled 0.5% higher at $62.59 a barrel.

Canada's government is in discussions with energy companies and the oil-producing province Alberta about eliminating a federal cap on emissions from the country's oil and gas sector if the industry and province reduce their carbon footprint in other ways, three sources with knowledge of the talks said on Thursday.
